commands.description: List directory contents. Often used with flags to see hidden files or file details.
commands.mistake: Running ls in a folder with millions of files without pagination or limit, causing the terminal output buffer to freeze.

commands.description: Search for files in a directory hierarchy based on names, types, sizes, or dates.
commands.mistake: Forgetting to wrap the name pattern in quotes, which causes the shell to expand the glob pattern locally before running find.

commands.description: Remove files or directories. Use recursive flag to delete folders.
commands.mistake: Running 'rm -rf /' or omitting path configurations, which can permanently wipe out the system filesystem.

commands.description: Change the access permissions of files or directories (read, write, execute).
commands.mistake: Setting 'chmod 777' on files in production, which gives global write and execute access, creating severe security vulnerabilities.

commands.description: Change file owner and group ownership of files and directories.
commands.mistake: Forgetting the -R flag when you want to change ownership for all nested files and subdirectories.

commands.description: Redirect command standard output (stdout) or standard error (stderr) to a file or stream.
commands.mistake: Using '>' when you mean to append. '>' will overwrite the file entirely, destroying previous logs. Use '>>' to append.

commands.description: Build and execute command lines from standard input (useful for chaining output files to arguments).
commands.mistake: Forgetting that xargs splits on spaces/newlines, which can fail or cause issues if filenames contain spaces. Use 'find -print0 | xargs -0' to solve this.

commands.description: Send a signal to a process (usually to terminate or kill it by PID).
commands.mistake: Using 'kill -9' (SIGKILL) immediately. Always try 'kill -15' (SIGTERM) first to allow the process to clean up connection resources and write files.

commands.description: Run a command immune to hangups, allowing background processes to continue running even after you log out of the SSH session.
commands.mistake: Forgetting to add '&' at the end, which keeps the process in the foreground instead of putting it into the background.
